past_medical_history: Iron-deficiency anemia, diagnosed 2 years ago, intermittently treated with oral iron supplementation
History of two cesarean deliveries (ages 30 and 34)
Uterine adhesions (Asherman syndrome) managed with hysteroscopic adhesiolysis 18 months ago
Generalized anxiety disorder, managed with counseling
Migraine without aura, approximately 2-3 episodes per year
No prior cardiac history, no known arrhythmias
family_history: Mother alive at age 64, has hypertension and type 2 diabetes
Father died at age 45 from sudden cardiac arrest while sleeping — no autopsy performed
Maternal grandmother died suddenly at age 52, presumed stroke but unconfirmed
One sister, age 41, has palpitations and was told she has a 'heart murmur' but has not undergone formal cardiac evaluation
No known family history of epilepsy or seizure disorders
No known family history of inherited connective tissue disorders

social_history.alcohol: Occasional glass of wine, approximately once per week
social_history.living_situation: Lives with husband and two children (ages 4 and 8) in suburban house
social_history.exercise: Walks 2-3 miles daily during lunch break, occasional weekend hiking

neurological_exam.mental_status: Alert, oriented to person, place, time, and situation. Glasgow Coma Scale 15. Speech is fluent with normal rate and prosody, no dysarthria or aphasia. Attention intact — able to spell 'WORLD' backward without error. Registration 3/3, recall 3/3 at 5 minutes. Mood appears anxious but cooperative. No confusion, no disorientation. Patient provides a coherent and detailed history.
neurological_exam.cranial_nerves: CN II: Visual fields full to confrontation bilaterally. Pupils equal, round, reactive from 4mm to 2mm bilaterally. Fundoscopic exam unremarkable, no papilledema. CN III/IV/VI: Extraocular movements intact in all directions of gaze, no nystagmus, no diplopia. CN V: Sensation intact to light touch and pinprick in V1, V2, V3 distributions bilaterally. Masseter and temporalis strength symmetric. Corneal reflexes intact bilaterally. CN VII: Face symmetric at rest and with activation, no upper or lower facial weakness. CN VIII: Hearing grossly intact to finger rub bilaterally. Weber midline, Rinne air > bone bilaterally. CN IX/X: Palate elevates symmetrically, uvula midline, gag reflex intact. CN XI: Sternocleidomastoid and trapezius strength 5/5 bilaterally. CN XII: Tongue protrudes midline without atrophy, fasciculations, or deviation.
neurological_exam.motor: Bulk normal throughout. Tone normal, no rigidity or spasticity. Strength 5/5 in all major muscle groups tested bilaterally: deltoids, biceps, triceps, wrist extensors, wrist flexors, finger extensors, grip, interossei, hip flexors, hip extensors, knee extensors, knee flexors, ankle dorsiflexors, ankle plantarflexors. No pronator drift. No tremor at rest or with action.
neurological_exam.sensory: Light touch, pinprick, vibration, and proprioception intact and symmetric throughout all four extremities and trunk. No sensory level. No dermatomal sensory deficits. Temperature sensation intact bilaterally.
neurological_exam.reflexes: Biceps 2+ bilaterally, triceps 2+ bilaterally, brachioradialis 2+ bilaterally, patellar 2+ bilaterally, Achilles 2+ bilaterally. Plantar responses flexor (downgoing) bilaterally. No clonus. No Hoffman sign bilaterally.
neurological_exam.coordination: Finger-to-nose testing accurate bilaterally without dysmetria or intention tremor. Heel-to-shin smooth and accurate bilaterally. Rapid alternating movements intact and symmetric.
neurological_exam.gait: Normal stride length, base, and arm swing. Tandem gait intact without difficulty. Romberg negative. No ataxia, no unsteadiness. Able to walk on heels and toes without difficulty.
neurological_exam.additional: No tongue laceration noted. No evidence of urinary or fecal incontinence. Mild tenderness over left lateral forehead where patient struck a desk during the most recent episode. Small superficial contusion noted at left temple, non-depressed. No raccoon eyes or Battle sign. No evidence of prolonged tonic-clonic activity.

patient.chief_complaint: Three episodes of sudden loss of consciousness over the past six weeks, most recently at work today with head injury from a fall
patient.history_present_illness: Ms. Maria Espinoza is a 38-year-old woman who presents to the emergency department after her third episode of sudden loss of consciousness in the past six weeks. She was brought in by ambulance from her workplace, an elementary school, where she collapsed while walking briskly up a flight of stairs carrying a stack of textbooks. According to a colleague who witnessed the event, Ms. Espinoza suddenly went pale, dropped the books, and fell forward, striking her left temple on the corner of a desk. The witness describes that after hitting the ground, Ms. Espinoza's body stiffened momentarily and her arms and legs jerked rhythmically for approximately 10 to 15 seconds. The witness initially believed she was having a seizure. Ms. Espinoza regained consciousness approximately 30 to 40 seconds after the event began and was immediately aware of her surroundings, asking 'What happened? Did I fall?' She was alert, oriented, and conversational within one minute. There was no post-event confusion, no repetitive questioning, and no drowsiness. She noted only a headache from the impact and mild nausea that resolved within ten minutes. The first episode occurred approximately six weeks ago while she was hiking uphill with her family on a moderately strenuous trail. She felt a sudden sensation of her heart 'fluttering' for a few seconds, then her vision went gray, and she lost consciousness. Her husband reports she was on the ground for approximately 20 seconds, turned 'white as a sheet,' and that her body appeared stiff briefly but he did not observe clear jerking. She recovered rapidly and was fully alert within 30 seconds. She attributed this to the heat, mild dehydration, and her history of anemia. The second episode occurred approximately two weeks ago while she was climbing stairs at home while carrying her 4-year-old child. She experienced sudden onset of lightheadedness followed immediately by loss of consciousness. She fell backward onto the stairs, and her husband caught the child. He describes her being unresponsive for about 15 to 20 seconds with brief stiffening of her limbs. Again, she recovered quickly with no post-ictal confusion. After this second episode, she consulted her primary care physician, who checked a hemoglobin level (11.2 g/dL, mildly low) and attributed the episodes to her ongoing anemia. Iron supplementation was increased. The patient reports that in retrospect, all three episodes occurred during some form of physical exertion — hiking uphill, climbing stairs with a load, and walking briskly up stairs. She denies any episodes at rest. She also reports approximately four to five episodes over the past two months of brief lightheadedness and a sensation of her heart 'skipping' or 'racing' for several seconds during exertion, which resolved spontaneously. She denies chest pain, sustained palpitations at rest, shortness of breath at rest, orthopnea, leg swelling, or exercise intolerance beyond the syncopal events. She denies any history of seizures, aura, tongue biting, or urinary incontinence during any episode. She is significantly anxious about the episodes, particularly given her father's sudden death at age 45, and wonders whether the events could be panic attacks. Her primary care physician had also raised the possibility of new-onset seizures and suggested neurological evaluation.

background.posterior_dominant_rhythm: 9.5 Hz alpha rhythm, symmetric, well-modulated, reactive to eye opening and closure
background.anterior_rhythms: Low-amplitude beta activity, symmetric, physiologically distributed, likely related to mild anxiety or escitalopram use
background.sleep_features: Drowsiness and stage II sleep captured with normal vertex waves and sleep spindles. No pathological sleep features.

artifacts.description: Intermittent muscle artifact, likely related to patient anxiety and tension, does not obscure underlying cerebral activity
Frequent eye blink artifact during wakefulness, expected and does not impair interpretation

activating_procedures.hyperventilation: Performed for 3 minutes. Symmetric, diffuse high-amplitude delta slowing during hyperventilation, which is a normal physiological response. No epileptiform discharges or focal features provoked. Patient reported mild lightheadedness during hyperventilation but no clinical event captured. Normal recovery within 60 seconds of cessation.
activating_procedures.photic_stimulation: Performed at frequencies 1-30 Hz. Symmetric photic driving response noted at 14-20 Hz. No photoparoxysmal response. No photoconvulsive response.

eeg.impression: Normal awake and asleep EEG study. No epileptiform discharges, no focal slowing, no electrographic seizures, and no subclinical seizure patterns identified during this 40-minute routine recording. The enhanced beta activity is consistent with benzodiazepine or SSRI medication effect and is not pathological. The normal interictal EEG does not completely exclude epilepsy; however, given the clinical description of rapid recovery, exertional triggers, and lack of post-ictal confusion, a cardiac etiology for the syncopal episodes should be strongly considered.
eeg.limitations: Routine 40-minute recording. Sensitivity of a single routine EEG for interictal epileptiform discharges is approximately 29-55%. No events were captured during this study. Prolonged ambulatory EEG monitoring could increase sensitivity if clinical suspicion for epilepsy persists.
recommended_actions: If epilepsy remains in the differential, consider prolonged ambulatory EEG or video-EEG monitoring to capture a habitual event
Given the exertional nature of episodes and family history of sudden cardiac death, cardiac evaluation including ECG, echocardiography, and ambulatory cardiac monitoring is recommended
Repeat EEG with sleep deprivation could be considered if epilepsy suspicion persists

findings: Normal sinus rhythm at 76 bpm
Normal P-wave morphology and axis
Normal PR interval
Narrow QRS complexes
QTc 448 ms — upper limit of normal, borderline
Three isolated premature ventricular complexes noted during the 10-second recording strip, with left bundle branch block morphology and inferior axis
No ST segment elevation or depression
No T-wave inversions in precordial leads
Nonspecific T-wave flattening in leads III and aVF
No Brugada pattern
No epsilon waves identified
No pre-excitation (no delta waves, no short PR)
ecg.interpretation: Twelve-lead ECG demonstrates normal sinus rhythm at 76 beats per minute with all intervals within normal limits, though the QTc of 448 ms is at the upper boundary of normal for a female patient (upper limit 460 ms). Three PVCs with LBBB morphology and inferior axis are noted, which may originate from the right ventricular outflow tract or the left ventricular summit. Nonspecific T-wave flattening in inferior leads is of uncertain significance. No overt conduction abnormalities, no pre-excitation, and no classic Brugada pattern identified. This resting ECG does not exclude a paroxysmal arrhythmic etiology for the patient's syncopal episodes.
ecg.clinical_correlation: The QTc of 448 ms, while technically within normal limits for females (<460 ms), warrants attention in the context of recurrent exertional syncope and family history of sudden cardiac death. Escitalopram (SSRI) can prolong QTc. Isolated PVCs on a resting ECG have limited diagnostic yield but combined with the clinical history, further evaluation with ambulatory cardiac monitoring, echocardiography, and potentially exercise stress testing is warranted.

CBC.clinical_significance: Mild anemia, consistent with known iron-deficiency anemia. Could contribute to exercise intolerance but unlikely sole cause of syncope.
Elevated, consistent with iron-deficiency anemia with anisocytosis

Cardiac_markers.clinical_significance: Normal, no evidence of acute myocardial injury
Normal, does not suggest significant heart failure
Normal, low probability of acute thromboembolism

Iron_studies.clinical_significance: Depleted iron stores, confirms iron-deficiency anemia
Markedly low, confirms functional iron deficiency

labs.interpretation: Laboratory evaluation reveals confirmed iron-deficiency anemia (hemoglobin 10.8 g/dL, ferritin 8 ng/mL, low serum iron with elevated TIBC and low transferrin saturation) with microcytosis. While anemia can contribute to exercise intolerance and might lower the threshold for syncope, isolated iron-deficiency anemia of this degree is generally not sufficient to cause recurrent exertional syncope with loss of consciousness. All electrolytes are normal, ruling out hypokalemia, hypomagnesemia, and hypocalcemia as proarrhythmic substrates. Normal troponin, BNP, and D-dimer do not suggest acute cardiac injury, heart failure, or pulmonary embolism. Normal glucose and TSH rule out hypoglycemia and thyroid dysfunction. The anemia findings represent a significant confounding factor that may distract from the underlying cardiac etiology, and iron deficiency should be treated regardless of the syncope workup outcome.

Echocardiogram.value: Normal, trileaflet, no stenosis or regurgitation
Mild mitral valve prolapse of the posterior leaflet with trivial mitral regurgitation
Echocardiogram.clinical_significance: Mild MVP noted; may be associated with arrhythmia in some cases. No hemodynamically significant regurgitation.

Echocardiogram.value: Normal size and function, TAPSE 22 mm

output.interpretation: Transthoracic echocardiogram demonstrates a structurally normal heart with preserved left ventricular systolic function (LVEF 62%). No hypertrophy, no wall motion abnormalities, and no significant valvular disease. Mild mitral valve prolapse of the posterior leaflet is noted with only trivial mitral regurgitation, which is hemodynamically insignificant. MVP is a common finding but has been associated with ventricular arrhythmias in a subset of patients, particularly when there is MAD (mitral annular disjunction). The structurally normal heart on echocardiography does not exclude primary electrical cardiac disease (channelopathies, catecholaminergic polymorphic VT) or mitral valve prolapse arrhythmic syndrome. Cardiac MRI may provide additional tissue characterization if clinical suspicion for arrhythmogenic substrate remains.
abnormal_values_summary: Mild posterior leaflet mitral valve prolapse with trivial MR

output.rhythm: Predominantly normal sinus rhythm with multiple episodes of non-sustained polymorphic ventricular tachycardia
findings: Predominant normal sinus rhythm with average rate 74 bpm (range 56-126 bpm)
Total of 1,247 PVCs in 72 hours (0.8% PVC burden), polymorphic with at least 3 distinct morphologies

Five runs of non-sustained ventricular tachycardia identified: 4 beats at 178 bpm (Day 1, 07:22 during morning walk), 6 beats at 195 bpm (Day 1, 14:45 during stair climbing at work), 9 beats at 210 bpm (Day 2, 08:30 during brisk walk), 5 beats at 188 bpm (Day 2, 16:12 during playground supervision), 14 beats at 225 bpm with bidirectional morphology (Day 3, 09:15 during stair climbing with heavy bag)
The longest NSVT run (14 beats) showed alternating QRS axis consistent with bidirectional ventricular tachycardia
All NSVT episodes occurred during documented periods of physical activity or emotional stress (diary correlation)
Isolated PVCs increase in frequency with heart rates above 100 bpm
No sustained VT captured, no VF
No significant bradycardia, pauses, or AV block
No atrial arrhythmias detected
QTc appears to shorten appropriately with exercise; no paradoxical QTc prolongation with increasing heart rate
output.interpretation: 72-hour Holter monitoring reveals catecholamine-sensitive ventricular ectopy with multiple runs of non-sustained ventricular tachycardia, all triggered by physical exertion or emotional arousal. The most significant finding is a 14-beat run of bidirectional ventricular tachycardia at 225 bpm during stair climbing. Bidirectional VT is a highly specific finding with a narrow differential: catecholaminergic polymorphic ventricular tachycardia (CPVT), digitalis toxicity, and rarely Anderson-Tawil syndrome. Given the absence of digitalis use and the reproducible exertional trigger, CPVT is the leading diagnostic consideration. The polymorphic PVC morphology and exercise-dependent escalation pattern further support this diagnosis.
output.clinical_correlation: The bidirectional VT on Holter monitoring is a critical diagnostic finding that substantially alters the diagnostic trajectory. CPVT is an inherited channelopathy with a high risk of sudden cardiac death, particularly during exercise or emotional stress. This finding explains the exertional syncope pattern and the family history of sudden cardiac death. Urgent exercise stress testing under monitored conditions is indicated to confirm the diagnosis by reproducing the arrhythmia. Genetic testing for RYR2 and CASQ2 mutations should be pursued. The patient should be restricted from exercise pending definitive diagnosis and treatment.

output.rhythm: Sinus rhythm at baseline with progressive ventricular ectopy culminating in bidirectional and polymorphic VT at peak exercise

intervals.QTc: 446 ms at rest, 432 ms at peak exercise (appropriate shortening)

findings: Resting ECG: Normal sinus rhythm at 72 bpm, rare isolated PVCs
Stage 1 (Modified Bruce): Heart rate 92 bpm, isolated PVCs emerge with bigeminal pattern
Stage 2: Heart rate 108 bpm, frequent PVCs, ventricular couplets and triplets with two distinct morphologies

Stage 3: Heart rate 126 bpm, runs of non-sustained bidirectional ventricular tachycardia (4-6 beat runs), classic alternating right and left bundle branch block morphology with beat-to-beat axis shift
Stage 4: Heart rate 148 bpm, sustained bidirectional VT degenerating into polymorphic VT lasting 22 beats at 240 bpm — test immediately terminated
Patient reported feeling 'dizzy and my heart is racing' at the onset of sustained VT, became pre-syncopal
Recovery: VT terminated within 6 seconds of exercise cessation and intravenous bolus of esmolol 500 mcg/kg. Sinus rhythm restored at 98 bpm. All ectopy resolved within 3 minutes of rest.
Blood pressure: 108/68 at rest, appropriate augmentation to 142/78 during sinus rhythm, dropped to 72/48 during VT run
No ischemic ST changes during sinus rhythm portions
The graded, reproducible escalation from isolated PVCs to couplets to bidirectional VT to polymorphic VT with increasing exercise intensity is pathognomonic for CPVT
output.interpretation: Exercise stress test confirms the diagnosis of catecholaminergic polymorphic ventricular tachycardia (CPVT). The hallmark stepwise progression of ventricular arrhythmia with increasing exercise workload — from isolated PVCs to couplets to bidirectional VT to polymorphic VT — is pathognomonic for CPVT and was fully reproduced. The bidirectional VT morphology (alternating RBBB and LBBB pattern with beat-to-beat axis shift) is the electrocardiographic signature of CPVT. The arrhythmia terminated promptly with exercise cessation and beta-blocker administration, consistent with catecholamine-dependent mechanism. No ischemic changes or QTc prolongation were observed, effectively ruling out coronary disease and long QT syndrome as alternative explanations.
output.clinical_correlation: This exercise stress test is diagnostic for CPVT. The patient must be immediately started on maximally tolerated beta-blocker therapy (nadolol preferred, as it has the best evidence in CPVT). Flecainide should be considered as adjunctive therapy. All strenuous exercise and competitive sports must be prohibited. Genetic testing for RYR2 (autosomal dominant, accounts for ~60% of CPVT) and CASQ2 (autosomal recessive, ~5% of CPVT) is essential. ICD implantation should be discussed given the syncope history, though beta-blocker therapy is the first-line treatment. First-degree relatives require cascade screening with exercise stress testing.

signal_characteristics.cine: Posterior leaflet prolapse of 3 mm beyond the annular plane in systole. Mild mitral annular disjunction (MAD) of 4 mm measured at the posterior insertion.

findings.location: Basal inferior and inferolateral left ventricular wall, at the papillary muscle insertion sites

signal_characteristics.T1: Mildly elevated native T1 values in the inferior wall (1085 ms, upper limit of normal ~1050 ms)
signal_characteristics.late_gadolinium_enhancement: Small focal areas of mid-wall late gadolinium enhancement at the basal inferolateral wall and inferior papillary muscle insertion — pattern compatible with MVP-related fibrosis or post-inflammatory scarring

additional_observations: No right ventricular fatty infiltration or wall motion abnormalities to suggest ARVC
No asymmetric septal hypertrophy to suggest HCM
Mitral annular disjunction (MAD) of 4 mm at the posterior annulus — this is a recognized substrate for ventricular arrhythmia in MVP patients
output.impression: Cardiac MRI demonstrates a structurally normal heart with preserved biventricular function. Mild posterior leaflet mitral valve prolapse is confirmed with associated mitral annular disjunction (4 mm). Small focal areas of late gadolinium enhancement are present at the basal inferolateral wall and papillary muscle insertion sites, a pattern that has been associated with arrhythmic MVP syndrome. However, the extent of fibrosis is limited and the LGE pattern is nonspecific. Importantly, there is no evidence of hypertrophic cardiomyopathy, ARVC, or infiltrative cardiomyopathy. In the context of the Holter findings demonstrating bidirectional VT and the exercise stress test confirming CPVT, the primary arrhythmic mechanism is likely CPVT (a primary electrical disease) rather than MVP-related arrhythmia, though the mild MVP and MAD may represent an incidental finding or minor additional arrhythmogenic contributor.
recommended_actions: Genetic testing for CPVT-associated genes (RYR2, CASQ2, TRDN, CALM1-3)
Initiate maximally tolerated beta-blocker therapy (nadolol preferred)
Cascade screening of first-degree family members with exercise stress testing
ICD evaluation given syncope and polymorphic VT on exercise testing
Restrict all strenuous exercise and competitive sports

differential_by_imaging.diagnosis: Structurally normal heart — primary electrical disease (CPVT) is the diagnosis
differential_by_imaging.rationale: No structural cardiomyopathy identified. The essentially normal cardiac MRI supports a diagnosis of primary electrical disease (channelopathy) as the cause of the ventricular arrhythmia.
Mild MVP with MAD and focal inferolateral LGE is present but is likely an incidental or minor contributing finding. The bidirectional VT pattern is pathognomonic for CPVT, not MVP-related arrhythmia.
The focal LGE could represent remote myocarditis, but no T2 edema, no wall motion abnormality, and the clinical picture does not support active or recent myocarditis.

Tilt_table_test.clinical_significance: Mild decrease but within normal limits
Borderline decrease, still within normal limits

Tilt_table_test.value: Mild lightheadedness at 30 minutes, resolved spontaneously
Not elicited after 45 minutes of tilt
Tilt_table_test.clinical_significance: No cardioinhibitory or vasodepressor response provoked

Tilt_table_test.value: Not administered — contraindicated given documented exercise-induced VT
Tilt_table_test.clinical_significance: Isoproterenol withheld due to known catecholamine-sensitive VT, which would be potentially dangerous

output.interpretation: Tilt table test is negative with no vasovagal response elicited during 45 minutes of passive tilt. Hemodynamic responses are appropriate with only mild physiological reduction in blood pressure. Isoproterenol provocation was appropriately withheld given the established diagnosis of catecholamine-sensitive ventricular arrhythmia. This result effectively rules out vasovagal syncope and orthostatic hypotension as the mechanism for the patient's syncopal episodes. The negative tilt table test is consistent with a cardiac arrhythmic etiology.

Arrhythmia_genetic_panel.clinical_significance: Pathogenic missense variant in RYR2, the most commonly affected gene in catecholaminergic polymorphic ventricular tachycardia type 1 (CPVT1). This variant has been previously reported in multiple unrelated CPVT families and is classified as pathogenic per ACMG criteria. It affects the central domain of the ryanodine receptor, leading to abnormal calcium release from the sarcoplasmic reticulum during adrenergic stimulation.

Confirms genetic diagnosis of CPVT type 1 (autosomal dominant). Cascade genetic testing of all first-degree relatives is strongly recommended, including the patient's sister who has reported palpitations and a heart murmur, and both of the patient's children.

output.interpretation: Comprehensive arrhythmia genetic panel identifies a pathogenic missense variant in the RYR2 gene (c.12475C>T, p.Arg4159Cys), confirming the genetic diagnosis of catecholaminergic polymorphic ventricular tachycardia type 1 (CPVT1). RYR2 mutations are responsible for approximately 60% of CPVT cases and follow autosomal dominant inheritance with variable penetrance. This finding explains the patient's exertional syncope and the family history of sudden cardiac death (the patient's father likely had undiagnosed CPVT). The patient's sister, who reports palpitations and a heart murmur, may also carry this variant and requires urgent cascade screening. Both of the patient's children (ages 4 and 8) have a 50% chance of inheriting the variant and require screening, initially with exercise stress testing and genetic testing.
abnormal_values_summary: Pathogenic RYR2 variant: c.12475C>T (p.Arg4159Cys) — CPVT type 1

interactions: Nadolol + Escitalopram: Both agents may prolong QTc interval. Monitor ECG when initiating combination. Risk is generally low with nadolol (non-selective beta-blocker) but warrants monitoring given borderline QTc at baseline.
Nadolol + Sumatriptan: Potential for enhanced vasospasm. Beta-blockers may increase sumatriptan levels. Use with caution; consider alternative migraine therapy.
contraindications: No absolute contraindications to nadolol in this patient
Relative caution: baseline heart rate 78 bpm and BP 108/68 — monitor for symptomatic bradycardia and hypotension with dose titration
warnings: Nadolol is the preferred beta-blocker for CPVT due to its non-selectivity, long half-life, and best evidence base (Hayashi et al., Circulation 2009)
Target dose: 1-2.5 mg/kg/day (approximately 60-150 mg daily for this patient)
Do not abruptly discontinue — risk of rebound catecholamine surge and arrhythmia
Iron-deficiency anemia should be corrected as it can exacerbate exercise intolerance and potentially lower the arrhythmia threshold
alternatives: Propranolol (non-selective, shorter half-life, requires multiple daily dosing — less preferred)
Flecainide (Class IC antiarrhythmic — add-on therapy if beta-blocker alone is insufficient; proven efficacy in CPVT as adjunct to beta-blocker)
Metoprolol and atenolol should NOT be used — selective beta-1 blockers are inferior to non-selective beta-blockers in CPVT and are associated with higher breakthrough event rates

output.query: catecholaminergic polymorphic ventricular tachycardia diagnosis management sudden cardiac death prevention

results.title: 2022 ESC Guidelines for the Management of Patients with Ventricular Arrhythmias and the Prevention of Sudden Cardiac Death
results.key_finding: CPVT diagnosis is based on exercise-induced bidirectional or polymorphic VT in the absence of structural heart disease. First-line therapy is non-selective beta-blocker (nadolol preferred). Flecainide is recommended as add-on therapy. ICD is indicated for cardiac arrest survivors and patients with syncope or sustained VT despite optimal medical therapy. All patients should avoid competitive sports and strenuous exercise.

results.title: Long-term outcomes of catecholaminergic polymorphic ventricular tachycardia: an international multicenter study
results.source: Journal of the American College of Cardiology
results.key_finding: In 327 CPVT patients followed for a median of 8 years, nadolol was associated with the lowest rate of cardiac events (5% at 5 years) compared to other beta-blockers (17%) and no treatment (40%). Combination therapy with nadolol plus flecainide reduced events further. Female sex, diagnosis after syncope, and RYR2 mutations were associated with better prognosis than CASQ2 mutations.

results.title: Efficacy of flecainide as adjunctive therapy in CPVT: a systematic review and meta-analysis
results.key_finding: Flecainide reduces exercise-induced ventricular arrhythmia burden by approximately 76% when added to beta-blocker therapy in CPVT. The mechanism involves direct inhibition of RYR2-mediated calcium release. Flecainide should be considered in all CPVT patients with breakthrough arrhythmias on beta-blocker monotherapy.

output.summary: CPVT is a rare inherited channelopathy (prevalence ~1:10,000) with a high risk of sudden cardiac death during exercise or emotional stress. The diagnostic hallmark is exercise-induced bidirectional or polymorphic VT in a structurally normal heart. Non-selective beta-blocker therapy (nadolol preferred) is the cornerstone of treatment, with flecainide as adjunctive therapy. ICD implantation is recommended for patients with syncope or cardiac arrest despite optimal medical therapy, though ICD shocks can paradoxically trigger catecholamine surges and arrhythmia storms. Exercise restriction and cascade family screening are essential components of management.

ground_truth.primary_diagnosis: Cardiac syncope due to catecholaminergic polymorphic ventricular tachycardia (CPVT type 1) with exercise-induced bidirectional and polymorphic ventricular tachycardia

differential.key_distinguishing: No post-ictal confusion (immediate return to full alertness within 30-60 seconds), no tongue biting, no urinary incontinence, normal EEG. The witnessed 'jerking' represents convulsive syncope from cerebral hypoperfusion, not epileptic seizure activity. Exertional trigger is atypical for epilepsy.

Documented iron-deficiency anemia (Hgb 10.8, ferritin 8) provides a convenient alternative explanation that was accepted by the PCP. However, anemia of this degree rarely causes true syncope with loss of consciousness. It may lower the threshold but does not explain the exertional pattern and convulsive features.

No clear vasovagal triggers (not triggered by pain, emotional stress, or prolonged standing), episodes occur exclusively during exertion, negative tilt table test. Age is slightly older than typical vasovagal syncope presentation.

Patient has known anxiety disorder which provides another red herring, but true syncope with LOC is extremely rare in panic attacks. Episodes have no preceding anxiety symptoms, occur during physical exertion, and have consistent reproducible features.

QTc is borderline at 448 ms but within normal limits for females. No paradoxical QTc prolongation with exercise. The exercise stress test pattern with bidirectional VT is pathognomonic for CPVT, not LQTS (which typically shows polymorphic VT/TdP without bidirectional morphology and often with pause-dependent onset).

Mild MVP with MAD found on echo and cardiac MRI, but the bidirectional VT morphology is specific to CPVT. MVP-associated arrhythmias typically present as monomorphic VT or frequent PVCs from the papillary muscles, not bidirectional VT.

optimal_actions.action: Obtain 12-lead ECG on presentation to assess for arrhythmia, conduction disease, or repolarization abnormalities
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Normal sinus rhythm with isolated PVCs and borderline QTc; resting ECG does not capture the paroxysmal arrhythmia

optimal_actions.action: Obtain comprehensive laboratory studies including CBC, BMP, cardiac markers, iron studies, and pregnancy test
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Iron-deficiency anemia identified (Hgb 10.8, ferritin 8) which serves as a confounding finding; normal cardiac markers and electrolytes

optimal_actions.action: Obtain EEG to evaluate for epileptiform activity given referral concern for possible seizures
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Normal EEG with no epileptiform activity, supporting cardiac rather than epileptic etiology

optimal_actions.action: Recognize that the anemia, while real, is insufficient to explain the clinical presentation — exertional syncope with convulsive features requires cardiac evaluation
Recognize that exertional syncope with a family history of sudden cardiac death demands urgent cardiac evaluation regardless of other findings
Order prolonged ambulatory cardiac monitoring (72-hour Holter monitor) to capture paroxysmal arrhythmia
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Multiple runs of NSVT including bidirectional VT during exertion, strongly suggestive of CPVT

optimal_actions.action: Obtain transthoracic echocardiogram to evaluate for structural heart disease
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Structurally normal heart with mild MVP — no HCM, no ARVC, ruling out structural causes

optimal_actions.action: Order exercise stress test under monitored conditions to reproduce the arrhythmia and confirm CPVT diagnosis
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Reproducible graded ventricular ectopy with bidirectional and polymorphic VT at peak exercise — diagnostic of CPVT

optimal_actions.action: Restrict patient from all strenuous exercise and physical exertion immediately
Initiate nadolol (non-selective beta-blocker) as first-line therapy for CPVT
Refer to inherited arrhythmia or channelopathy center for genetic testing and long-term management
Recommend cascade screening of first-degree family members, especially the sister with palpitations and both children

critical_actions: Recognize that exertional syncope with convulsive features is not necessarily epilepsy and requires cardiac evaluation
Do NOT attribute the syncope solely to iron-deficiency anemia — anemia at this level does not cause recurrent exertional syncope with LOC
Identify the family history of sudden cardiac death (father at age 45) as a major red flag for inherited cardiac disease
Do NOT start antiepileptic medications based on the presumptive seizure diagnosis
Order ambulatory cardiac monitoring and exercise stress testing to capture and reproduce the arrhythmia
Restrict the patient from strenuous exercise and physical exertion pending cardiac evaluation
Initiate non-selective beta-blocker therapy (nadolol preferred) once CPVT is diagnosed — do NOT use selective beta-1 blockers
Refer for genetic testing and cascade family screening
contraindicated_actions: Initiating antiepileptic drugs for presumed seizures without adequate cardiac evaluation
Attributing all episodes to iron-deficiency anemia and discharging with iron supplementation alone
Allowing the patient to continue strenuous exercise or hiking pending workup
Prescribing a selective beta-1 blocker (metoprolol, atenolol) instead of a non-selective beta-blocker for CPVT — selective agents are inferior and associated with breakthrough events
Administering isoproterenol for tilt table provocation in a patient with documented catecholamine-sensitive VT
Dismissing the family history of sudden cardiac death as unrelated
Performing lumbar puncture (no indication for CNS pathology)
Prescribing stimulants or sympathomimetic agents that could trigger arrhythmia
key_reasoning_points: Convulsive syncope (brief myoclonic jerks from cerebral hypoperfusion) is frequently misdiagnosed as epileptic seizures — the rapid recovery without post-ictal confusion is the key distinguishing feature

Iron-deficiency anemia serves as a major diagnostic red herring in this case — it provides a convenient, seemingly sufficient explanation that satisfies Occam's razor but fails to account for the exertional pattern and severity of the episodes
The exclusive exertional trigger for all syncopal episodes is the most critical clinical clue pointing toward a cardiac arrhythmic etiology, specifically a catecholamine-sensitive arrhythmia

A normal resting ECG does NOT exclude dangerous paroxysmal arrhythmias — CPVT patients characteristically have normal resting ECGs because the arrhythmia is only triggered by catecholamine surges during exercise or emotional stress
Family history of sudden cardiac death at age 45 in the father is a critical red flag for an inherited cardiac condition, particularly when combined with the patient's sister having palpitations

Bidirectional ventricular tachycardia on Holter monitoring or exercise testing has a very narrow differential (CPVT, digitalis toxicity, Anderson-Tawil syndrome) and is essentially pathognomonic for CPVT in the absence of digitalis use
The stepwise progression of ventricular ectopy with increasing exercise intensity (PVCs → couplets → bidirectional VT → polymorphic VT) is the hallmark diagnostic finding of CPVT
Non-selective beta-blockers (nadolol) are specifically indicated in CPVT and superior to selective beta-1 blockers — prescribing the wrong class of beta-blocker is a potential management error
The mild MVP with MAD found on cardiac MRI is a secondary finding that could mislead toward MVP arrhythmic syndrome, but the bidirectional VT morphology is specific to CPVT

metadata.difficulty_description: This case is a diagnostic puzzle because: (1) the patient is referred with a presumptive diagnosis of seizures due to witnessed convulsive features, (2) documented iron-deficiency anemia provides a compelling alternative explanation that was already accepted by the PCP, (3) the resting ECG is normal with only incidental PVCs, (4) the patient has comorbid anxiety disorder which provides yet another potential explanation, (5) the critical diagnostic clue (bidirectional VT) is only revealed on follow-up ambulatory monitoring and exercise stress testing, not on initial workup, and (6) an incidental finding of MVP with MAD on cardiac MRI could redirect toward a different arrhythmic diagnosis
clinical_pearls: CPVT has a prevalence of approximately 1:10,000 and is one of the most lethal inherited channelopathies, with untreated mortality rates of 30-50% by age 40
Bidirectional VT (beat-to-beat alternation of QRS axis) is pathognomonic for CPVT and is virtually diagnostic when seen during exercise testing
RYR2 mutations account for approximately 60% of CPVT cases and follow autosomal dominant inheritance with age-dependent penetrance
Nadolol is the preferred beta-blocker for CPVT based on its non-selectivity, long half-life, and superior clinical evidence compared to other beta-blockers

Iron-deficiency anemia can lower the threshold for arrhythmias by reducing oxygen delivery to the myocardium, potentially contributing to the severity of episodes but not causing the underlying arrhythmogenic substrate
diagnostic_traps: Witnessed convulsive features (stiffening and jerking) leading to misdiagnosis as epileptic seizures
Documented iron-deficiency anemia providing a seemingly sufficient alternative explanation accepted by the PCP
Normal resting ECG creating false reassurance about cardiac etiology
Patient's anxiety disorder and escitalopram use suggesting psychogenic or drug-related cause
Borderline QTc potentially misdirecting toward long QT syndrome rather than CPVT
Incidental MVP with MAD on cardiac MRI potentially misdirecting toward MVP arrhythmic syndrome
